THE PULSE

April 2026  •  Issue #1
From the Desk of Product

The Pulse is officially live. Issue #1, fresh off the press and we couldn't be more excited to share it with you. We do a lot of great work that doesn't always get the visibility it deserves, and that ends now. This is a monthly space to share what we've been building, celebrate the people doing the work, and keep everyone in the loop.

Product Updates

What we shipped

Analytics  •  March 2026
  • Maternity Care Management Model — NICU/AI: The Data Science Team & Clinical Operations Team began work on a joint initiative to address NICU Admissions. The objective is to build a model to predict risk of NICU admission, with a particular focus on predicting and preventing high-severity long NICU stays & those complicated by morbidity and adverse outcomes. Together, the teams built a model that has demonstrated ability to predict risk for adverse NICU outcomes early in pregnancy, and are working with the Technology team to deploy a prototype for further evaluation.
Technology  •  March 2026
  • SDoH Enhancements Impacting the Product: In March, we completed work to deliver SDoH (Social Determinants of Health) scoring using public data sources. This new capability provides scored insights across the following key categories: Medical Debt, Food Access, Maternity, English Language Proficiency, Single-Parent Households, No High School Diploma, Housing Cost Burden, Group Quarters, Unemployed, No Vehicle, and Crowding. In addition, we implemented SDoH risk values tied to ICD-coded risks, enabling better identification and stratification of patient-level social risk factors directly within the Product. These enhancements strengthen our ability to incorporate non-clinical factors into risk assessment, care planning, and member support workflows — directly improving the depth and actionability of insights available in the Product.
What's Next

Looking ahead

Sprint 8  •  April 6–17, 2026
  • High-Risk SDoH Discover feature roll out to Humana and CareFirst on 4/17
Sprint 9  •  April 20–May 1, 2026
  • Staging Rewrite: Improve maintainability, scalability, and stability of staging processes and architecture

Lucina Care: Working on building out a full 12 month postpartum program and building out a full provider enablement playbook that includes onsite and virtual care management support
Accolades

Spotlights & shoutouts

Name Role Recognition
Dan Hebert Lead Data Engineer Dan's proposed project, a new Preterm Birth Risk Awareness Tool, has been selected as one of the final 3 in the Unified IT Shark Tank Hackathon! The concept is a simple, anonymous web-based survey where any pregnant woman can enter basic pregnancy and health information along with her zip code. No PHI is collected or stored; except the zip and that is accounted for. The tool would generate an estimated preterm birth risk level (high, medium, low) not the score. This is clearly positioned as an educational resource to help women have more informed conversations with their doctors. This project leverages our existing AI strengths at Lucina Analytics (part of Unified Women's Healthcare) to raise awareness of our mission, introduce our capabilities to more providers and patients, and create a voluntary, fully anonymized data flywheel that could help strengthen our risk models over time. Demo Lucina Glow at: Lucina Glow
Maksim Akhmetshin Sr Testing Engineer Maksim built out the Databricks testing framework, delivered and operationalized a comprehensive unit and data integration testing framework, strengthening quality, consistency, and confidence across data engineering workflows.
Jodi Majors Director Clinical Program Management & Innovation Jodi has been instrumental in the Lucina Care Development, as well as, creating a lot of policies and care management guides.
Lindsay's Process Corner

From the process desk

Story Points
Here's something new: story points. We're moving away from hour estimates (which, let's be honest, were mostly fiction) and toward complexity-based planning using the Fibonacci scale — 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13, 21. Bigger gaps mean bigger unknowns, and that's intentional. During planning we'll vote simultaneously using Teams polls so no one anchors the group. Actual effort in hours still matters for capitalizable work, but points are locked once planning ends. Lindsay will be scheduling a practice estimation session for later this week and you are encouraged to join if you are new to estimating in points or even a point warrior. Want to know more? The full process doc is located here: Story Point Estimation Process
Azure DevOps (ADO) Work Item Workflow
If you've ever wondered what all those work item types in ADO actually mean, here's the short version. Epics are the big-picture stuff, think quarters-long initiatives that live on the roadmap. Features break those down into real deliverables, and then User Stories, Bugs, Research, and Design items are what the team actually picks up sprint to sprint. From there, work flows through a clear set of statuses from Backlog all the way to Closed, with checkpoints at Dev, QA, and UAT before anything hits production. Sprints are two weeks: start committed, end clean. Detailed ADO Workflow

New Faces

Alan Meert
Director of Data Science
I live in Philadelphia, PA with my wife and 2 sons (5 and 8). I have been in the machine learning/data science field for 10 years. I studied physics and astronomy for undergrad and grad school. In my free time, I enjoy math/science/engineering projects like brewing my own beer, tinkering with home automation, and building legos with my kids.
☕ Coffee, tea, or chaos (energy drinks)?
I choose Coffee first but will drink any of the three
📱 Do you close your apps when you're done, or let them pile up?
I let my apps pile up. Same with tabs in the browser.
